text: On right thoracoscopy, you can see a large mass with an adherent right upper lobe lung.
superior vena cava and the phrenic nerve are visualized proximally and distally to the mass.
To gain access to the mass and the phrenic nerve, we first performed a wedge resection of the right upper lobe that was adherent to the mass.
This allowed us to retract the mass to the left side of the chest, thus trying to begin the first part of the operation, which was freeing up the left phrenic nerve, which was imminently
associated with the large mass.
Of note, the mass had a large inflammatory component, making the dissection somewhat more challenging.
Here, the left phrenic can be seen being carefully teased away using the ligature device.
We approached this inferiorly to superiorly as we were approaching the superior vena cava.
After we knew we could free up the phrenic nerve, we then proceeded to try to free up the mass on the left side of the chest.
Knowing that it was a large inherent mass, we got into the pericardium and used this as a guide to help the resection, aiming toward the left phrenic and the inanimate subclavian junction.
As we're proceeding up toward the inanimate vein on the left side, we decide to go superiorly so that we can define the superior and inferior aspects of the more dangerous part of the resection.
Here we can be seen freeing the anterior and left superior horn of the thymus and the mass.
We're approaching the left pleura and the lung can clearly be seen on the left side and we use this as a guide to resect the pericardium and protect the left phrenic.
We enter the left pleura enabling us to rotate the mass around and see the
margin of the left phrenic to protect that during this dissection.
Grabbing the pericardium we rotate the mass to the right exposing the left phrenic and the margins of the mass on the left side.
It is freed thus easily coming up the pericardium and now superiorly on top of the inanimate vein which is the most difficult part of the margin.
We then spend the next few minutes dissecting out proximally and distally and superiorly to the inanimate vein.
Of note, there are some large collaterals because of the superior venocaval obstruction.
The internal mammary vein on the left is a nice landmark to dissect along the inanimate vein.
at the superior portion of where the mass is mostly adherent to the superior vena cava and phrenic.
Because of the size of the mass, we work inferiorly and posteriorly to free it up to be able to rocket off the inanimate vein.
You can see here now we're approaching the inanimate vein from below now that we have isolated it from above and can safely approach it from below.
Having freed it up on the left side of the phrenic, we are now freeing it up superiorly above the inanimate vein.
You can see a large collateral because of the obstructing mass that had developed over time.
The mass is freed from the large collateral and the inanimate vein becomes easier to dissect upon.
We then will work on the inferior portion of the inanimate vein from inside the pericardium.
Finally, as we approach from inferiorly, we enter more of the pericardium and are able to free the inferior portion of the inanimate vein from the mass.
The mass did have somewhat of an inflammatory characteristic as described below, making the dissection somewhat more challenging than usual.
Here we can see using the aortic arch as a guide.
Entering the pericardium early in the case greatly facilitates the resection.
The mass is then put into a bag and removed through a counter incision along the ninth rib.
Here you can see the final anatomy with the atrium, aorta, and right ventricle.
On the back table, the mass is entered, and you can see the contents of a large teratoma, almost under pressure.
Gallbladder, pancreas, and other structures can be seen, including a small hair.
